cmd:
rm -f /tmp/kvwal.wal /tmp/kvwal.wal.sync && ./kvwal serve &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 0.5
echo "Writing one key..."
./kvwal client write-batch 1
kill -9 $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null
wait $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true
echo "Checking sync file..."
ls -la /tmp/kvwal.wal*
echo "Content of sync file (hexdump):"
hexdump -C /tmp/kvwal.wal.sync 2>/dev/null || echo "No sync file"
echo "Size of WAL file:"
ls -l /tmp/kvwal.wal

^I^I}$
^I^Ikey, value := kv[0], kv[1]$
$
^I^I// Write to WAL first M-bM-^@M-^T this is the "durable" path.$
^I^I// BUG: Append only buffers the data; fsync happens in the$
^I^I// background. But we send +OK immediately, leading the client$
^I^I// to believe the write is durable.$
^I^Iif err := s.wal.Append(key, value); err != nil {$
^I^I^Ireturn fmt.Sprintf("-ERR wal: %v", err)$
^I^I}$
$

^I^I// the safe truncation point.$
^I^Is.writeS

cmd:
cd /workspace
rm -f /tmp/kvwal.wal /tmp/kvwal.wal.sync
pkill -f kvwal 2>/dev/null || true
sleep 0.5

# Start server
./kvwal serve > server.log 2>&1 &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 0.5

# Write 5 entries
ACKED=$(./kvwal client write-batch 5 2>&1)
echo "Acked keys: $ACKED"

# Kill server
kill -9 $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null
wait $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true

# Crash recover
./kvwal crash-recover 2>&1

# Restart server
./kvwal serve > server2.log 2>&1 &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 0.5

# Verify
RESULT=$(./kvwal client

cd /workspace
rm -f /tmp/kvwal.wal /tmp/kvwal.wal.sync
pkill -f kvwal 2>/dev/null || true
sleep 0.5

# Start server
./kvwal serve 2>&1 &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 1

# Write 5 entries
ACKED=$(./kvwal client write-batch 5)
echo "Acked keys: $ACKED"

# Kill server
kill -9 $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null
wait $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true

# Crash recover
./kvwal crash-recover

# Restart server
./kvwal serve 2>&1 &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 1

# Verify
./kvwal client verify-batch "$ACKED"

kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/nu
